thread.title: Newcastle Rising Tide protests draw thousands to Foreshore Park as flotilla launched
thread.title_full: Newcastle Rising Tide protests draw thousands to Foreshore Park as flotilla launched

author: Simon McCarthy
title: Newcastle Rising Tide protests draw thousands to Foreshore Park as flotilla launched
text:
The [Eternal Bliss](https://www.newcastleherald.com.au/story/8826588/watch-as-coal-ship-eternal-bliss-sails-past-newcastle-harbour-climate-protesters/) sailed unobstructed into the heads on Thursday evening, past a sentinel of a handful of kayaks who remained on the water as the numbers dwindled around 5pm from the protesters' first paddle out.
She sailed past thousands on the same beach on Saturday afternoon, sitting low in the water, carrying more than 97,294 tonnes of Hunter Valley export. No one stood in her way.
The 229-metre bulk carrier was one of over 30 to depart the harbour during the week-long climate action protest staged by [the activist group Rising Tide](https://www.newcastleherald.com.au/story/8824432/inside-the-rising-tide-newcastle-harbour-protest-in-november-2024/). Four more were due to arrive after 2pm on Saturday, six on Sunday, and eight would depart before the week's end.
In a statement earlier this week, [the NSW Port Authority said,](https://www.newcastleherald.com.au/story/8827110/coal-shipping-in-newcastle-port-continues-despite-rising-tide-climate-protest/) despite the protest, port operations would continue for as long as safety requirements were met.
Organisers estimated that as many as 3000 people had joined the action in Foreshore Park on Saturday, November 23, where Midnight Oil frontman Peter Garrett performed an acoustic set with the band's guitarist Martin Rotsey and The Jezabels' keys player Heather Shannon.
"Citizen action is not going away," Mr Garrett said after stepping off stage as the boats were carried to the water again. "It will continue to build and be part of the solution."
Onstage, the Australian protest songwriter and activist turned one-time Labor politician lashed the "overreach from the authorities, including the NSW Government" in clamping down on the action in the months and weeks leading up to the event as "unnecessary". It was a statement met with cheers and shouts of "shame".
"I mean, seriously, is the threat to the wellbeing and the peace of the Hunter region to be found in a group of citizens exercising their democratic rights peacefully, or is it to be found in continuing to export a material that is going to blow the world up in a furnace?" he said. "Let's get our perspectives sorted out here."
"Australians are jack of us not doing something about climate," he said later. "The jobs are in renewable energy; the healthy climate is in renewable energy. The vitality of regions is in renewable energy. And the fossil fuel industry is desperately hanging on to a business model that sees it producing greater levels of pollution into the atmosphere, making it all the harder to stabilise the climate."
"I have probably seen my most active days as a musician, an activist and a politician, but that doesn't mean all of the generations coming behind me won't have to deal with the problem at a greater intensity than I've had to. The longer we leave it, the harder and more expensive it is. So, let's get on with it."
Police had made no arrests during the week to the weekend's mass demonstration, though protesters claimed on Saturday afternoon that as many as eight were detained by officers and returned to shore without charge when they tried to paddle through police lines.
A high-visibility enforcement operation involving mounted and seafaring officers, police on the ground, traffic and highway patrol, aerial support, and the K-9 unit continued over the weekend.
Rising Tide, in a statement, identified two protesters they say were detained as Newcastle neurologist Peter Schofield and 16-year-old Melbourne High School student Myles Wilkinson. Footage released by the protesters shows a man being helped out of a kayak into a police vessel, while in another, two protesters raise their fists in their kayak as an officer on board a jet ski holds their boat in position.
NSW Police have been contacted for comment.
The amassed crowd that spilled through Foreshore Park at the weekend heard from a roster of speakers, including Mr Garrett, who on more than one occasion invoked comments from Opposition Leader Peter Dutton, who has previously [touted the Hunter Valley as a nuclear energy hub](https://www.newcastleherald.com.au/story/8706642/peter-dutton-pushes-for-nuclear-at-liddell-site-despite-agls-stance/) in place of renewables.
Mr Garrett referred to his 'nuclear-powered harmonica' calling it "irrational" among other things, and Greens senator Mehreen Faruqi, in her speech, appeared to call back to a 2015 incident in which Mr Dutton, then the Immigration Minister, was caught on a hot mic joking about "water lapping at the door" of Pacific Island nations.
"The climate crisis is engulfing not just people's lives, livelihoods and homes here, but the global south and particularly our Pacific neighbours who did nothing to create this crisis, and are seeing water lapping on their doorsteps," Ms Faruqi said. "They are on the front lines of this crisis, and their future homes and dreams are being crushed."
"As a wealthy colonial country and as one of the biggest dealers in coal and gas, it is this government's responsibility to act strongly and end new coal and gas."
The details of this report are developing. It may be updated.
